column1: 27-year-old man after high-speed blunt trauma Q7M2K8P4Z1XA
column3:
AP chest radiograph of a 27-year-old man involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision shows rightward shift of the upper trachea , a contour abnormality and widening of the upper mediastinum , a large left hemothorax and associated left apical cap, wide left intercostal spaces, and rightward mediastinal shift.
Coronal CECT of the same patient shows the underlying traumatic aortic injury characterized by an intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, and a large contained rupture .
column5:
Traumatic aortic injury with contained rupture

Caption-derived keyword findings:
rightward shift of the upper trachea
contour abnormality and widening of the upper mediastinum
large left hemothorax
associated left apical cap
intimomedial flap
contour irregularity
large contained rupture

Tight reasoning:
The radiograph shows multiple indirect signs of mediastinal hemorrhage after high-speed trauma, and the coronal CECT supplies the direct diagnostic signs: intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, and contained rupture. This matches traumatic aortic injury, a high-mortality injury classically occurring at fixed aortic segments after rapid deceleration.

Differential:
Ductus diverticulum
Atherosclerotic ulceration
Motion or pulsation artifact

[2] Coronal CECT of the same patient shows the underlying traumatic aortic injury characterized by an intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, and a large contained rupture .
column21:
🩻 Traumatic Aortic Injury

Traumatic aortic injury is a high-mortality aortic wall injury, usually from high-velocity blunt deceleration trauma, with CTA as the key diagnostic test and treatment-planning study.
Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer synthesis; Core facts retrieved from Core Radiology Vascular Imaging pages 547-549 and IR page 604, synthesized with the provided RadPrimer Traumatic Aortic Injury article.

🧾 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • TAI = traumatic aortic injury.
  • Synonyms/acronyms in the article include ATAI, BTAI, BAT, BAI, BTAR, aortic transection, and traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm.
🧠 Summary
  • TAI ranges from intimomedial injury to transection and is most commonly caused by blunt rapid deceleration injury such as MVC or fall from height greater than 10 feet; penetrating trauma is less common in the RadPrimer article.
  • The most common injury site is the aortic isthmus at/near the ligamentum arteriosum; RadPrimer states 90%, and Core also emphasizes the isthmus as the most common location.
  • Other vulnerable fixed levels include the aortic root and diaphragmatic hiatus.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • Radiography: wide mediastinum and aortic contour abnormality are major indirect clues; normal chest radiograph is exceedingly rare in RadPrimer, with NPV 98%.
  • Other radiographic signs include left apical cap, obscured descending thoracic aorta, inferior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus, tracheal/tube deviation, widened paravertebral/paratracheal stripes, AP window obscuration, and first rib fracture as a severe-trauma clue.
  • CTA: modality of choice/gold standard in both RadPrimer and Core; RadPrimer gives sensitivity 98% and specificity 80%.
  • Direct CTA signs include intimomedial flap, irregular aortic contour, abrupt caliber change, pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture, aortic wall disruption, intramural hematoma, and rarely dissection or active extravasation.
  • Indirect CTA sign is periaortic or mediastinal hemorrhage; Core emphasizes that hemorrhage touching the aortic wall suggests aortic injury, while hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by a fat plane can be presumed venous.
  • Morphologically normal aorta with no mediastinal hematoma has 100% NPV for excluding TAI in RadPrimer.
⚖️ Subtype / Risk Framework
  • SVS Grade I = intimal injury/minimal aortic injury; routinely nonoperative with close monitoring and CTA follow-up.
  • Grade II = intramural hematoma; Grade III = pseudoaneurysm; Grade IV = rupture.
  • Minimal aortic injury is a subcentimeter intimomedial abnormality without external contour deformity; RadPrimer states 10% of ATAI, while Core states approximately 10-30% of blunt traumatic aortic injuries.
🔍 Differential Diagnosis
  • Wide mediastinum false positives include rotation, supine positioning, expiratory imaging, mediastinal fat, and tortuous great vessels.
  • CTA mimics include ductus diverticulum, fusiform proximal descending aortic enlargement, aortic spindle, atherosclerotic ulceration/plaque, bronchial-intercostal trunk infundibulum, adjacent opacified vessels, and pulsation/motion/streak artifacts.
  • Ductus diverticulum favors a smooth anteromedial isthmus outpouching with gently sloping shoulders, no intimomedial flap, and no adjacent hemorrhage.
🛠️ Clinical / Treatment Pivots
  • No specific or sensitive clinical signs occur until hemodynamic instability; chest pain or dyspnea may occur.
  • Adequate blood pressure control is paramount; RadPrimer states rupture risk decreases from 12% to 1.5%.
  • Anti-impulse therapy for grade I injuries uses IV beta-blocker; RadPrimer target: SBP ≤100 mm Hg, MAP ≤80 mm Hg, HR ≤100 bpm.
  • TEVAR is recommended over open repair for suitable anatomy and is used for grade II-IV injuries in RadPrimer; open repair is reserved when anatomy precludes TEVAR.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Pseudoaneurysm or contained rupture should not imply stability.
  • Chronic pseudoaneurysm may appear as a peripherally calcified AP-window/middle mediastinal mass with thrombus.
  • MR generally has no role in acute trauma, but may follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
  • TEE can show intimal tear, transection, or hemopericardium and is most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
⭐ Super Summary
  • In major blunt trauma, screen the radiograph for mediastinal hemorrhage clues, then rely on CTA/MPRs to prove direct aortic wall injury, usually at the isthmus, and separate true injury from ductus diverticulum, artifact, plaque, branch-vessel infundibulum, and venous mediastinal hemorrhage.

column1: 12-year-old boy after high-speed blunt trauma L4N8V2Q9C6TR
column3:
AP chest radiograph of a 12-year-old boy involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision shows a wide mediastinum, mild rightward displacement of endotracheal and gastric tubes secondary to aortic injury, and mediastinal hemorrhage. Note concurrent right pleural effusion and bilateral pulmonary contusions .
Sagittal CECT of the same patient shows the classic location of aortic injury at the isthmus with focal aortic disruption and pseudoaneurysm formation.
column5:
Traumatic aortic injury at the aortic isthmus with pseudoaneurysm

Caption-derived keyword findings:
wide mediastinum
mild rightward displacement of endotracheal and gastric tubes
mediastinal hemorrhage
classic location of aortic injury at the isthmus
focal aortic disruption
pseudoaneurysm

Tight reasoning:
The frontal radiograph provides indirect hemorrhage clues, while the sagittal CECT localizes the direct aortic wall injury to the isthmus with focal disruption and pseudoaneurysm. The isthmus is the classic location for deceleration-related aortic injury in both Core and RadPrimer.

Differential:
Ductus diverticulum
Aortic spindle
Branch vessel infundibulum

column1: 43-year-old woman after motor vehicle collision A9T3X7M2Q6LP
column3:
Axial CECT of a 43-year-old woman involved in a motor vehicle collision shows an intimomedial flap at the aortic isthmus, associated intramural hematoma , and concurrent left hemothorax.
Parasagittal CECT of the same patient shows the location of the aortic injury at the aortic isthmus with resultant focal outpouching , consistent with pseudoaneurysm formation, which results as the aortic adventitia "contains" the focal aortic disruption, but should not imply stability.
column5:
Traumatic aortic injury with intimomedial flap, intramural hematoma, and pseudoaneurysm

Caption-derived keyword findings:
intimomedial flap
aortic isthmus
associated intramural hematoma
concurrent left hemothorax
focal outpouching
pseudoaneurysm formation
aortic adventitia "contains" the focal aortic disruption

Tight reasoning:
The axial CECT shows direct aortic injury signs at the isthmus, including intimomedial flap and intramural hematoma, and the parasagittal image shows a focal outpouching consistent with pseudoaneurysm. The caption emphasizes the key reporting pitfall: adventitial containment should not be interpreted as stability.

Differential:
Aortic dissection
Ductus diverticulum
Atherosclerotic ulceration

column1: 65-year-old man with incidental thoracic opacity K2R8P5N1X7QA
column3:
Lateral chest radiograph of an asymptomatic 65-year-old man shows a rounded peripherally calcified opacity in the middle mediastinum.
Axial CECT of the same patient shows a focal pseudoaneurysm at the level of the aortic isthmus with peripheral calcification and eccentric intraluminal thrombus, corresponding to the radiographic abnormality. Upon further inquiry, the patient reported a history of trauma due to a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 years previously.
column5:
Chronic post-traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm

Caption-derived keyword findings:
rounded peripherally calcified opacity
middle mediastinum
focal pseudoaneurysm
aortic isthmus
peripheral calcification
eccentric intraluminal thrombus
history of trauma due to a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 years previously

Tight reasoning:
The radiograph shows a calcified middle mediastinal/AP-window-type opacity, and CECT confirms a focal isthmic pseudoaneurysm with peripheral calcification and thrombus. The remote high-speed trauma history supports a chronic post-traumatic pseudoaneurysm, which RadPrimer highlights as a diagnostic consideration when vascular calcification is seen at the AP window.

Differential:
Calcified lymph node
Thoracic aortic aneurysm
Mediastinal mass

column5:
Traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm

Caption-derived keyword findings:
blunt chest trauma
contour irregularity
focal aortic pseudoaneurysm
adjacent mediastinal hemorrhage
distinguish injury from branch vessel infundibulum

column1: 58-year-old man after blunt chest trauma V8C3M1Q6R9TX
column3: Sagittal CECT of a 58-year-old man who sustained blunt chest trauma shows a smooth caliber change of the aortic isthmus adjacent to a calcified ligamentum arteriosum . Absence of adjacent hemorrhage should prompt consideration of ductus diverticulum.
column5:
Ductus diverticulum mimicking traumatic aortic injury

Caption-derived keyword findings:
smooth caliber change
aortic isthmus
calcified ligamentum arteriosum
absence of adjacent hemorrhage
ductus diverticulum

Tight reasoning:
The key discriminator is that the isthmic contour change is smooth and occurs adjacent to a calcified ligamentum arteriosum, with no adjacent hemorrhage. RadPrimer lists ductus diverticulum as a top mimic and describes it as smooth, gently sloping, and without an intimomedial flap.

Differential:
Traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm

column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: this is a smooth isthmic contour variant without adjacent hemorrhage, rather than an irregular post-traumatic pseudoaneurysm with hematoma.
Ductus diverticulum: smooth caliber change/outpouching near the ligamentum arteriosum, no hemorrhage, no intimomedial flap.
Traumatic pseudoaneurysm: irregular contour or saccular outpouching with direct wall injury and often periaortic/mediastinal hemorrhage.
Atherosclerotic ulceration: irregular or ulcerated plaque, more typical in older patients and not simply a smooth ductus bump.

column1: 65-year-old man with incidental thoracic opacity K2R8P5N1X7QA [DD3]
column3:
Lateral chest radiograph of an asymptomatic 65-year-old man shows a rounded peripherally calcified opacity in the middle mediastinum.
Axial CECT of the same patient shows a focal pseudoaneurysm at the level of the aortic isthmus with peripheral calcification and eccentric intraluminal thrombus, corresponding to the radiographic abnormality. Upon further inquiry, the patient reported a history of trauma due to a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 years previously.
column7: Radiograph and CECT chest. Based on the images, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Chronic post-traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm
Thoracic aortic aneurysm
Calcified mediastinal lymph node
column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: the task is a chronic calcified AP-window/middle mediastinal opacity, not acute mediastinal hemorrhage.
Chronic post-traumatic pseudoaneurysm: focal isthmic pseudoaneurysm with peripheral calcification, eccentric thrombus, and remote trauma history.
Thoracic aortic aneurysm: broader aortic dilatation rather than focal post-traumatic outpouching at the isthmus.
Calcified mediastinal lymph node: nodal calcification does not opacify as a focal aortic outpouching on CECT.

column11: Why is the aortic isthmus the classic site of traumatic aortic injury?
column12: Core and RadPrimer both localize traumatic aortic injury to relatively fixed aortic levels, especially the isthmus near the ligamentum arteriosum. During rapid deceleration, mobile and fixed aortic segments move differently, concentrating shear, torsion, stretching, and hydrostatic forces at anchoring points such as the ligamentum arteriosum, root, and hiatus. RadPrimer also lists osseous pinch and water-hammer effects as contributing mechanisms. The imaging consequence is direct wall injury at these fixed levels, classically an intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, pseudoaneurysm, or intramural hematoma at the isthmus.

column11: Why does a traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm represent contained rupture rather than a stable lesion?
column12: RadPrimer defines traumatic pseudoaneurysm as a saccular outpouching contained only by adventitia; Core similarly describes post-traumatic pseudoaneurysm as contained rupture held by surrounding connective tissues. In the wall-injury ladder, intimomedial disruption progresses beyond a simple intimal lesion so blood escapes the normal lumen and is temporarily confined by the outer wall/adventitia or adjacent mediastinal structures. On CTA this produces a focal outpouching/contained rupture, but the containment mechanism is weak relative to a normal aortic wall. Therefore the report should not imply stability when describing pseudoaneurysm or contained rupture.

column13: In blunt chest trauma, what trap occurs when mediastinal hemorrhage is present but the aortic wall appears intact?
column14:
Trap: assuming every traumatic mediastinal hematoma equals aortic injury.
Safety check: trace whether hematoma contacts the aortic wall and look for direct CT signs: intimal irregularity/flap, pseudoaneurysm, dissection, intramural hematoma, active extravasation, or contour disruption.
Key discriminator: Core states mediastinal hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by a fat plane can be presumed venous, whereas hemorrhage in contact with the aortic wall suggests aortic injury.
Why it happens: traumatic mediastinal hemorrhage is often venous, but missing TAI is catastrophic.

column13: In trauma CTA, what trap occurs at the aortic isthmus when a smooth outpouching is seen?
column14:
Trap: calling a ductus diverticulum a traumatic pseudoaneurysm.
Safety check: assess margin morphology, intimal flap, and adjacent hemorrhage.
Key discriminator: ductus diverticulum has smooth, gently sloping shoulders and no intimomedial flap; absence of adjacent hemorrhage should prompt consideration of ductus diverticulum.
Why it happens: both lesions project near the aortic isthmus/ligamentum arteriosum, the classic site of true traumatic injury.

column13: What reporting trap should be avoided when describing traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm or contained rupture?
column14:
Trap: using wording that implies the lesion is stable because the rupture is contained.

Key discriminator: pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture is SVS grade III traumatic aortic injury, not a benign contour variant.
Why it happens: the word 'contained' can falsely sound reassuring despite ongoing rupture risk.

  • Morphologically normal aorta with no mediastinal hematoma has 100% NPV for excluding TAI in RadPrimer.

column15: How often do minimal aortic injury lesions occur in blunt traumatic aortic injury according to Core versus RadPrimer?

column15: What chest radiograph findings should trigger further investigation for traumatic aortic injury?

Wide mediastinum/aortic contour abnormality, AP-window/descending aortic obscuration, tracheal/tube deviation, left apical cap, widened paravertebral/paratracheal stripes, inferior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus, or first rib fracture.

column15: What is the nonoperative treatment pivot for minimal aortic injury in RadPrimer?
column16:
Grade I/minimal aortic injury: close monitoring and CTA follow-up with anti-impulse control.
RadPrimer target: SBP ≤100 mm Hg, MAP ≤80 mm Hg, HR ≤100 bpm.

column15: Which traumatic aortic injury grades are treated with TEVAR according to RadPrimer?
column16:
TEVAR: all grade II-IV injuries when anatomy is suitable.
Recommended over open repair for all age groups with suitable anatomy.
May require stent coverage of the left subclavian artery.

column15: When is open surgical repair used for traumatic aortic injury according to RadPrimer?
column16:
Open repair: only if anatomy precludes TEVAR.
RadPrimer notes increased risk of cord ischemia and death: 14% paraplegia, 20% overall mortality.
Exam pivot: open repair is not the default when TEVAR anatomy is suitable.

column15: What CTA features favor ductus diverticulum over traumatic pseudoaneurysm?
column16:
Ductus diverticulum: anteromedial isthmus outpouching with smooth, gently sloping shoulders and no intimomedial flap.
Absence of adjacent hemorrhage supports the mimic rather than injury.
Exam pivot: morphology + no hematoma prevents overcalling TAI.

  • Synonyms/acronyms in the article include ATAI, BTAI, BAT, BAI, BTAR, aortic transection, and traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm.
🧠 Summary
  • TAI ranges from intimomedial injury to transection and is most commonly caused by blunt rapid deceleration injury such as MVC or fall from height greater than 10 feet; penetrating trauma is less common in the RadPrimer article.
  • The most common injury site is the aortic isthmus at/near the ligamentum arteriosum; RadPrimer states 90%, and Core also emphasizes the isthmus as the most common location.
  • Other vulnerable fixed levels include the aortic root and diaphragmatic hiatus.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • Radiography: wide mediastinum and aortic contour abnormality are major indirect clues; normal chest radiograph is exceedingly rare in RadPrimer, with NPV 98%.
  • Other radiographic signs include left apical cap, obscured descending thoracic aorta, inferior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus, tracheal/tube deviation, widened paravertebral/paratracheal stripes, AP window obscuration, and first rib fracture as a severe-trauma clue.
  • CTA: modality of choice/gold standard in both RadPrimer and Core; RadPrimer gives sensitivity 98% and specificity 80%.
  • Direct CTA signs include intimomedial flap, irregular aortic contour, abrupt caliber change, pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture, aortic wall disruption, intramural hematoma, and rarely dissection or active extravasation.
  • Indirect CTA sign is periaortic or mediastinal hemorrhage; Core emphasizes that hemorrhage touching the aortic wall suggests aortic injury, while hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by a fat plane can be presumed venous.
  • Morphologically normal aorta with no mediastinal hematoma has 100% NPV for excluding TAI in RadPrimer.
⚖️ Subtype / Risk Framework
  • SVS Grade I = intimal injury/minimal aortic injury; routinely nonoperative with close monitoring and CTA follow-up.
  • Grade II = intramural hematoma; Grade III = pseudoaneurysm; Grade IV = rupture.
  • Minimal aortic injury is a subcentimeter intimomedial abnormality without external contour deformity; RadPrimer states 10% of ATAI, while Core states approximately 10-30% of blunt traumatic aortic injuries.
🔍 Differential Diagnosis
  • Wide mediastinum false positives include rotation, supine positioning, expiratory imaging, mediastinal fat, and tortuous great vessels.
  • CTA mimics include ductus diverticulum, fusiform proximal descending aortic enlargement, aortic spindle, atherosclerotic ulceration/plaque, bronchial-intercostal trunk infundibulum, adjacent opacified vessels, and pulsation/motion/streak artifacts.
  • Ductus diverticulum favors a smooth anteromedial isthmus outpouching with gently sloping shoulders, no intimomedial flap, and no adjacent hemorrhage.
🛠️ Clinical / Treatment Pivots
  • No specific or sensitive clinical signs occur until hemodynamic instability; chest pain or dyspnea may occur.
  • Adequate blood pressure control is paramount; RadPrimer states rupture risk decreases from 12% to 1.5%.
  • Anti-impulse therapy for grade I injuries uses IV beta-blocker; RadPrimer target: SBP ≤100 mm Hg, MAP ≤80 mm Hg, HR ≤100 bpm.
  • TEVAR is recommended over open repair for suitable anatomy and is used for grade II-IV injuries in RadPrimer; open repair is reserved when anatomy precludes TEVAR.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Pseudoaneurysm or contained rupture should not imply stability.
  • Chronic pseudoaneurysm may appear as a peripherally calcified AP-window/middle mediastinal mass with thrombus.
  • MR generally has no role in acute trauma, but may follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
  • TEE can show intimal tear, transection, or hemopericardium and is most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
⭐ Super Summary
  • In major blunt trauma, screen the radiograph for mediastinal hemorrhage clues, then rely on CTA/MPRs to prove direct aortic wall injury, usually at the isthmus, and separate true injury from ductus diverticulum, artifact, plaque, branch-vessel infundibulum, and venous mediastinal hemorrhage.

column15: What imaging appearance should raise chronic post-traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm?
column16:
Peripherally calcified mass/opacity at the aorticopulmonary window or middle mediastinum.
CECT/CTA: focal isthmic pseudoaneurysm with peripheral calcification and possible thrombus.
Exam pivot: ask about remote trauma when vascular calcification is seen at the AP window.

column15: What is the role of MR and TEE in acute traumatic aortic injury evaluation according to RadPrimer?
column16:
MR: generally no role in acute trauma; may identify/follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
TEE: can demonstrate intimal tear, transection, or hemopericardium; most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
Exam pivot: CTA remains the primary diagnostic tool.
